questions.prompt: How does Tailwind decide which utilities to include in the final CSS?

choices.text: JIT (just-in-time) compiler scans the files configured in `content` for class string literals, then generates CSS only for classes it found. No 'purge' step — utilities you never write never exist. Scan uses regex on source files; doesn't evaluate JavaScript, doesn't parse comments. Implication: dynamic class names (`bg-${color}-500`) aren't visible to the scanner — won't generate. Use full class strings in source code, or add to `safelist` in config for unavoidable dynamic cases. Final CSS is typically 10-100KB for even large apps because only used utilities ship.

questions.explanation: JIT scans for class strings, generates only what it found. Dynamic strings don't generate.

questions.prompt: CSS bundle is 200KB and growing. Audit shows hundreds of arbitrary values like `p-[13px]`, `bg-[#FF5733]`. Implication?

choices.text: Design system smell — your scale doesn't have what designs need, so the team escapes to arbitrary values everywhere. Each unique arbitrary value generates its own class, bloating output. Two fixes: (1) ADD the values to tailwind.config so the scale covers actual needs (e.g., add `13` and `15` to spacing if those keep appearing), and (2) audit the underlying design decisions — are these arbitrary values intentional or drift from the system? Often it's drift; conversation with design fixes the root cause. Bundle size shrinks naturally as you replace arbitrary values with scale steps that reuse.

questions.explanation: Lots of arbitrary values = design system gap. Add to config; question the underlying drift.
